g.text: Central Limit Theorem and Confidence Limits for Unknown Mean

text.tspan: The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that the distribution of sample means will be approximately normal, regardless of the original distribution of the population.
Let X1, X2, ..., Xn be a random sample of size n from a population with mean μ and variance σ^2. The sample mean is defined as:

tspan.data-narration: The CLT states that: n(X̄ - μ)^2 / σ^2 → χ^2(n) as n → ∞
Properties of the Central Limit Theorem
Asymptotic Normality: The distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normal, regardless of the original distribution of the population.

text.tspan: Asymptotic Normality: The distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normal, regardless of the original distribution of the population.

tspan.data-narration: Consistency: The sample mean will converge to the population mean as the sample size increases.

text.tspan: Consistency: The sample mean will converge to the population mean as the sample size increases.

tspan.data-narration: Independence: The sample mean is independent of the sample variance.

text.tspan: Independence: The sample mean is independent of the sample variance.
A confidence interval for the population mean is a range of values within which the true population mean is likely to lie.

tspan.data-narration: The confidence interval is calculated using the sample mean and sample standard deviation: X̄ ± (Z * (σ / √n))
Applications of the Central Limit Theorem

text.tspan: The CLT has numerous applications in statistical inference, including:

tspan.data-narration: Confidence intervals for the population mean.

text.tspan: Confidence intervals for the population mean.
Learn more about the Central Limit Theorem and its applications.
